fact: June 16 is exactly 167 days into the year, which means there are only 198 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this date typically falls in the middle of peak firefly season - millions of tiny lights are flickering in backyards everywhere!
The sun rises before 5:30 AM in most northern cities on June 16, making it one of the earliest sunrise days of the entire year.
June 16 sits right in the heart of Gemini season, when the zodiac twins are supposedly making everyone extra chatty and curious about everything.
In Antarctica, June 16 brings some of the longest, darkest nights of winter - the sun barely peeks above the horizon for just a few hours!
Most strawberry plants in North America are producing their juiciest, reddest berries right around this date every year.
The summer solstice is only 5 days away from June 16, making it one of the closest non-solstice days to the longest day of the year.
In many parts of northern Europe, June 16 often falls during white night season, when the sky never gets completely dark and stays a twilight blue all night long.
Cicadas that emerge every 17 years have historically peaked around mid-June dates like the 16th, creating ear-splitting bug concerts that can be heard from miles away.
Ocean water temperatures in the Northern Hemisphere are climbing fast by June 16, but they're still about 2-3 weeks away from their warmest point of summer.
June 16 falls during the time when Earth is actually moving toward its farthest point from the sun, even though it's getting hotter in the north - weird but true!
In the Southern Hemisphere, June 16 often brings some of the chilliest morning frosts of winter, with temperatures dropping to their lowest points before dawn.
